briefing.opening: Good morning/afternoon, everyone. I will now begin the climate briefing for 2026-06-15.

sections.title: 🔴 Deepening Climate Crisis and Widespread Impacts
sections.content: The world is facing increasingly severe climate impacts, with unseasonably early heatwaves causing serious concern. Daytime temperatures in Seoul have risen to 32 degrees Celsius, leading to an 80% increase in heat-related illnesses compared to the same period last year, posing a direct threat to public health and working environments [35, 67]. Beyond these immediate health crises, climate change is reshaping ecosystems and natural phenomena, from shifts in plant flowering times revealed by AI research to the migration of armadillos into the Appalachian region, and even traces of climate change found in champagne [37, 41, 42]. The 2026 World Cup faces heightened risks from dangerous heat, with studies indicating an increased heatwave risk in 49 matches, underscoring the urgent need for a 'war on heat' [7, 49, 65]. Economically, a majority of Americans feel that climate change is making their cost of living more expensive, and Pakistani mango farmers are also being hit by reduced exports due to climate change and Middle East tensions, demonstrating widespread economic impacts [39, 58]. Furthermore, critical infrastructure and natural environments are becoming vulnerable, with increased risks of torrential rains and wildfires in the Gyeongbuk region, and melting ice on Mount Everest revealing the bodies of over 200 climbers, highlighting the profound and extensive challenges of a changing climate [2, 3, 63, 66].

sections.title: 🇰🇷 Climate Adaptation Efforts and the Need for Infrastructure Reconfiguration
sections.content: Amidst these increasing risks, efforts are underway domestically and internationally to adapt to the changing climate. Jangheung County is exploring agricultural solutions suitable for the era of climate change, while Daejeon City is enhancing its climate crisis response with advanced technology [1, 10]. In particular, there is a strong demand to shift national infrastructure investment related to climate change from a recovery-centric approach to a prevention-centric one. Furthermore, it has been argued that current carbon-neutral focused energy policies are insufficient, and the balance of nuclear power, LNG, and renewable energy must be re-evaluated from a 'survival perspective' [4, 14, 28]. In areas like Masanhappo-gu, public-private partnerships are supporting vulnerable populations to have a healthy summer amidst heatwaves, and international adaptation efforts are also continuing, with approximately 1.2 billion VND allocated to climate change adaptation projects in Vietnam's Quang Tri province [9, 22]. Ecological and cultural adaptation cases are also gaining attention, such as the climate change adaptation methods of Australian native bees, research suggesting that ground bees may be better equipped for global warming, and the Wisconsin Art and Peony Festival developing new plans in response to climate change [36, 55, 64]. However, declining domestic travel demand, the emergence of climate change as a major issue, and the perception that traditional laws are lagging behind climate change realities underscore the urgency of comprehensive and forward-looking adaptation strategies [8, 59].

sections.title: 🌱 Spreading Carbon Neutrality Practices and Citizen Participation
sections.content: Positive momentum is building in carbon neutrality efforts, with SBTi (Science Based Targets initiative) demonstrating global impetus for stronger corporate action by releasing 'Corporate Net-Zero Standard 2.0,' which expands carbon removal and strengthens corporate accountability for implementation [11]. Domestically, community-led carbon reduction activities are actively unfolding, from the Gwangju Climate and Energy Agency's 'Carbon Neutral Earth Love Camping' for multicultural families to Jinju City's and Anseong City's 'Carbon Neutral Practice Walking Challenges,' achieving both health and environmental goals simultaneously [12, 13, 16, 17]. Local governments like Geoje City are actively encouraging energy saving, having distributed over 55 million KRW in carbon neutrality points to approximately 6,700 households, while SSG.com is offering discount coupons to promote the purchase of eco-friendly products [15, 19, 25, 18]. Furthermore, various organizations such as the Dongjak-gu Carbon Neutrality Alliance, Suncheon Office of Education, and Geochang-gun Saemaul Women's Association are conducting environmental cleanup and awareness improvement campaigns, and the HIRA (Health Insurance Review and Assessment Service) is also actively promoting youth environmental education and carbon neutrality practice projects [20, 21, 23, 24, 26]. These widespread efforts, including a resident-participatory flea market in Soha 1-dong, Gwangmyeong City, demonstrate a growing collective will to achieve carbon neutrality through tangible daily actions, and news that UK electric vehicle drivers are saving significant annual costs highlights the economic benefits of the eco-friendly transition [27, 72].

sections.title: 🌍 Accelerating Renewable Energy Transition and Global Trends
sections.content: The global transition to renewable energy continues to accelerate. Switzerland has taken a bold step towards energy independence by passing legislation targeting 38 TWh of renewable energy by 2026, and South Korea is also accelerating its goal of achieving 100 GW of renewable energy, creating new opportunities for solar, ESS (Energy Storage System), and grid companies [29, 30]. To support this transition, the Korea Power Exchange's Central Headquarters has opened in Osong, Chungbuk, to respond to the expansion of renewable energy [31]. However, a report indicating that South Korea's solar power generation cost is twice the international average suggests that challenges remain for competitive renewable energy deployment [32]. Beyond the energy sector, broad discussions on climate change are taking place globally, with lectures by Nobel laureates in economics and student art exhibitions raising climate change awareness. At the KTH alumni event in Dubai, AI, new and renewable energy, and smart grids were among the main topics [33, 45, 69]. Scientific research is deepening our understanding by exploring approaches and challenges in attributing climate change impacts. Social and political aspects are also highlighted, such as the exclusion of indigenous peoples from climate response efforts, the complex sociopolitical dynamics in Central America where climate change and gangs facilitate migration and authoritarianism, and online debates sparked by Dia Mirza's remarks [38, 43, 44, 47, 48, 68].

briefing.closing: Today's briefing has once again reminded us of the severity of the climate crisis, along with domestic and international adaptation and carbon neutrality efforts, and the importance of transitioning to renewable energy. This is a time that demands continued attention and action. This concludes today's climate briefing.
